content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Religious movie you didn't realize was a religious movie?

**Score:** 137
**Body:** When I first saw *Book of Eli* I went in expecting a straight post apocalyptic action flick starring Mr. Training Day himself. I was taken by surprise that the big important plot device he was carrying was, in fact, the Bible. The bigger twist at the end made the movie more meaningful. Are there more movies like this where you go into it expecting one thing, but it ends up having more spiritual or religious themes?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Contact
2. Star Wars.

Turned out to be about some hokey religion and ancient weapons.
3. Signs - 2002
  - This was my first thought! And Knock At The Cabin too
4. Might be obvious but The Green Mile

Main characters initials are JC, he performs miracles. He is wrongly condemned to death which he accepts. When we see his body, the electrocution has left a scar similar to the crown of thorns.
5. Big Lebowski …Dudeism …has a lot to do with Buddhism and Taoism😎
  - And nihilism
    - That's just, like, your opinion, man.
6. It's not a movie, but Battlestar Galactica. As I was watching it, I started thinking: hold on, there's something odd going on here. Then I looked it up and apparently it was meant to be a Sci-Fi retelling of the Book of Mormon.

I eventually stopped watching it because it started not appealing to me as much, although it wasn't bad so maybe I'll try to finish the series sometime.

Edit: To clarify, I was watching the 2004 reboot not the 1978 original (which I didn't even know existed until now).
7. Narnia. All of them.

## 2. Idiocracy is absolutely brilliant (and also very terrifying). I also have to say, Justin Long is such an underrated actor.

**Score:** 53
**Body:** This was such a hilarious and well thought out movie. I can't believe it took me so long to watch it. 

Also, LOVED Justin Long's portrayal as a doctor. After watching him in all his different movies including, Jeeper's Creepers, The Wave,  Zac and Miri Make a Porno (his most iconic role, IMO), I have to point out his incredible range. He played so many different characters across different genres (very well-executed) and I really don't think he gets the recognition he deserves. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lol don't forget galaxy quest! He was great in Idiocracy 
  - I haven't watched Galaxy quest yet (I know, I know 🤦‍♂️). It's on the list.
    - Yeah I’m not gonna be that guy but you should definitely watch Galaxy Quest. I prefer Idiocracy but they’re almost equally as funny.
2. He made me literally lol in Barbarian
3. You have to watch Accepted then. Justin Long at his finest
  - I really love this movie and wish I could enroll in South Harmon Institute of Technology. 

He's got that Ferris Bueller thing, Blake Lively is really likable, Lewis Black is the perfect IDGAF professor and who doesn't love Robin Lord Taylor in anything?!?
4. I heard Vince tell a story about Dodgeball. When he came on set to get reaction shots to being hit by dodgeballs the machine that shot the balls was set way too strong and he asked them to turn the speed down because no one watching the movie would be able to tell how fast the balls were going. The crew told him that Justin had been taking hits from it to do his scenes. True dodgeball pro.
5. Great movie. I think a remake would be better now that Mike Judge is more established and would merit a better budget, distribution. It could be even better!

Justin Long good in Waiting as well.
6. His cameo in Zach and Miri is still one of my favorite comedy scenes ever
7. Go away, Batin.

## 3. 8 year oldest first Halloween "scary movie"?

**Score:** 51
**Body:** My son is 8 years old and requesting to watch horror movies. Obviously I don't want to traumatize him, but I also don't want it to be so boring he doesn't want to watch anymore. I tried suggesting kid ones like Monster House and Goosebumps and he's just like "ehh..." So I'm wondering what we could watch that won't be TOO much for him. I grew up watching Saw and Friday the 13th and that might be a little far for what I'm looking for

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tremors
  - Heck yes, this is just scary enough. It's also one of those "perfect movies" with a tight script that is still very fun to watch as an adult... and they do say a few naughty words at the beginning like "damn" and "shit" which will honestly just make an 8-year-old feel like they're watching something forbidden... and will heighten the scare factor.
2. Hocus Pocus

(although you might get asked what a virgin is)
  - Ya know. I dont think I ever questioned that as a kid. Just full flew over my head 🤣
3. The Mummy (1999). Scary but not terrifying, lots of action, good story, and the good guys win. Having a cat in the house helps too.
4. Is the Christina Ricci Addams Family (1 and 2) good enough? Not really horror but getting there. If he doesn’t like old movies though might not like it lol
5. The Monster Squad
6. Casper
7. Abbott and Costello Meet Frankenstein'
8. Casper!

The Addams family

Hocus Pocus

Frankenweenie

Corpse bride

The witches

The haunted mansion 


Less Halloweeny…

The nightmare before Christmas 

Beetlejuice? 

Ghostbusters 

Teen wolf

Coraline

## 4. Movies that have warm comfy vibe like big daddy(1999)?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** It’s my go to movie for when I’m sick. Looking for movie that’s positive but doesn’t need to be 100% kid friendly. A cast that just seems to mesh well. I’m especially fond of 90’s to 2000’s era because reminds me of my childhood.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Addams Family. The one with Raul Julia and Christopher Lloyd. THE MAMUSHKA!
2. The wedding singer
3. Heavyweights (1995)
  - That and holes are the only vhs tapes I ever wore out
4. Hook
5. - *Be Kind Rewind* ('08)
- *The House Bunny* ('08)
- *Role Models* ('08)
6. All the Adam Sandler movies seem to do the trick for me. Little Nicky is a personal fave. 
7. Grown Ups 1&2
8. Pay it Forward
9. Wayne's world

Tommy Boy

## 5. Movies that are genuinely confusing and not just a matter of paying attention, knowing context or watching a second time?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I feel like most movies that are considered "confusing" are just a matter of people not paying enough attention to it, lacking context, or are easily explained by someone else. Nolan movies (except Tenet, shoutout to that one because it baffled me, although not in the best way), The Lighthouse, Blade Runner, Donnie Darko, Stalker etc.

I want movies that are confusing because they're either convoluted and complex (but not in a bad way, of course), vague to the point it's truly hard to make heads or tails of it, or deeply experimental.

A few examples of movies and others that actually confused me:

Primer, Timecrimes, Predestination (yes, there's a theme here -- although for Predestination, it was more about the specifics of the time travel than how they reached the conclusion).

Serial Experiments Lain

Lynch movies (I've only watched Rabbits, BUT I expect the rest to confuse the heck out of me too).

I'm open to literally any kind of movie, just tell me that one movie that still keeps you up at night because you couldn't get it even after watching a 2 hour breakdown and analysis of it on youtube.



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. - *The Big Sleep* ('46)

At one point during the production, Bogart asked Howard Hawks, "Does Marlowe know who killed this guy, at this point in the script?" After some discussion they realized *they* didn't know themselves, so they went to the author of the book (and screenplay?) Raymond Chandler, who eventually confirmed he didn't know, either.

Meanwhile, it's a mystery whose main features are prostitution, homosexuality, and drug addiction, but in the movie they can't say anything about drugs, gayness, sex... But the movie is entertaining as hell scene to scene.
2. Mulholland Drive, famously.
  - Unfortunately I can't post an image here, I've made a screenshot of this thread with the Mulholland drive thread just underneath my feed :))
3. Synecdoche, NY. It’s a great movie that is also a great confusing mess.
4. Beau is Afraid

Inherent Vice

Any Jorodowsky movie

But then again, I guess I’m just listing surrealist films.. they’ll confuse the hell outta ya tho. 

Also I still don’t know wtf Syriana is about after having watched it twice. Phoenician Scheme too, all Wes Anderson’s new movies stories suck.
5. Pi (1998)

Enemy (2013)

Annihilation (2018)
6. Under the Silver Lake

So many hidden meanings, connections and allegories to consider
7. I watched Mulholland Drive after many edibles. It was the best thing I've ever seen, but I couldn't explain that movie even if I tried.
8. Men by Alex Garland
9. There's stuff like Under The Skin that isn't very complicated but is very open to interpretation

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Movies no one expected to be as good as they were?

**Score:** 8223
**Body:** Don’t get me wrong, I thought Logan was going to be a good movie but I never knew it was going to be a masterpiece. I’m not even a huge fan of the superhero genre but Logan is in a league of its own. Brutal action, great character development and a genuinely compelling story. 

What other movies did no one expect to be as good as they were? 

https://filmwaffle.com/post/logan-2017-the-most-violent-superhero-movie

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Starship Troopers
2. The Emperor's New Groove

Honestly one of Disney's best IMO,. But it went through development hell and hit theaters with very little promotion. Pretty sure it earned it's love through video store rentals. I know I picked it up out of boredom and was then completely blown away.
3. The Lego Movie
4. Galaxyquest. Had very low expectations and was blown away by it.
  - Its hilarious that Tony Shaloub's character was 

A. Faking being east-asian 

B. Blatantly stoned for most of the movie
    - Stoned, but they very carefully do not mention weed or anything else in exposition.  When I first saw it, I guess I missed that part and couldn't understand why he was acting so weird.  When I saw it again later, it made sense, but it's still odd that he's so obviously stoned, but it's never really mentioned.

From what I understand, they took out any mention of it in the editing room to get a PG rating.
5. Big Trouble in little China
6. Goon
  - Man I wish I could see this one for the first time again. I went in completely blind and I was blown away by this one. 

“Two rules, don’t touch my Percocets, and do you have any percocets”
7. Dredd

## 2. Tremors(1990) This is one of those movies that doesn't waste a second from beginning to end.

**Score:** 1862

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fun fact: Every actor in this film is only one degree of separation from Kevin Bacon
  - Well, except 1
2. It has no right to be as good as it is.

If you've never seen it, don't look up the plot or anything like that. Just watch it.

It's one of those films that people make posts about saying, "everyone said I should watch this, but I didn't until today and omg why did I wait so long?!"

Just watch it. You have the time.
  - That's the thing about it, and back when, long ago this movie was a daily watch. R.I.P Fred Ward.
3. It really did pick the wrong god damned rec room…
4. I remember a redditor writing that his movies teacher was telling the class that this movie was a good example of one where there are no unnecessary scene throughout its run
5. Perfect Blend of Horror and Comedy 

It keeps you tense with giant, underground monsters while sprinkling in witty dialogue and absurd situations that never feel forced.

Relatable Characters 
Val and Earl are everyday guys handymen, small-town folks who are likable, funny, and resourceful. You genuinely root for them instead of just watching “heroes” go through motions.

Creative Monster Design 
The Graboids aren’t just scary; their underground attacks and unique movement make them genuinely original threats, even decades later.

Suspense and Pacing 
The movie knows exactly when to ratchet tension and when to let the humor breathe. Each attack feels earned, and every moment in the desert keeps you on edge.

Memorable Setting 
The isolated desert town adds to the sense of danger and makes the stakes feel real. You can almost feel the sand and the emptiness, which heightens suspense.

Practical Effects that Still Hold Up 
 The physical creature effects, rather than CGI, give the Graboids a tangible presence that makes encounters feel visceral.

Charm and Heart 
It’s not just monsters and jokes there’s genuine camaraderie, romance, and small-town heart that makes the stakes matter emotionally.

In short, Tremors hits every note: suspense, laughs, creative monsters, and real characters, making it a rollercoaster from start to finish.
  - There’s also a meta joke with Michael Gross playing the prepper gun nut when he was most famous at the time for playing the liberal hippie dad on _Family Ties_. At the time it was really funny that he was playing “against type.” But 35 years later that’s something a lot of people won’t know.
6. We just like don’t vibrate
7. The script is absolutely perfect. Pretty much everything is set up within the first 15 minutes. The fence that they work on is the one they run alongside when they're being changed. They run into the college student who tells them there are weird vibrations in the area so it gives the viewer an idea of what's going on in the area. They pass by the construction crew that gets attacked later. They introduce the bulldozer that will come in handy later. Most of the town folk are introduced at the store so you know who to keep track of. And then when they leave town and the guys are talking to Nancy you see her daughter on her pogo stick so that way it doesn't suddenly come out of nowhere when the monster attacks. This is how movies should be made and it's way smarter than people give it credit for.

## 3. In your opinion why didn't Michael Jai White become the next big action star?

**Score:** 685

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Doesn't matter. We got Black Dynamite and that's all that's important.
  - Black Dynamite is legitimately one of the most clever comedies I have ever seen. It's a spoof on the Blaxploitation genre while also being an absurd love letter at the same time. It's full of all kinds of absolutely insane little details and references in it. The scene where Bullhorn hits the stunt man and it cuts to a different stunt person kills me every time. 

Lets not forget how amazing the Black Dynamite series on Adult Swim was too. His beef with OJ Simpson made my boomer dad laugh his ass off.
    - There is no better spoof than a loving one
  - > from now on, I'm declaring war on those who sell drugs in our community.

> But black dynamite! I sell drugs to the community!
2. Because the type of action movies he would have been prime star material for had been killed off by the time he truly came into his prime.


In the 80's he would be right next to Van Damme. In the 90's though Hollywood decided that kind of action movie/star was done and we were moving on to scrawny boys like Keanu and Will Smith.
  - It's unfortunate, because the change was basically to stop teaching fighters to act and start teaching actors to fight. Michael Jai White is one of the few (along with Scott Adkins and a select few others) who is a fighter who can act. He's been in a ton of stuff with no action at all, and created the absolute classic Black Dynamite. Just a shame that the type of movie he can really make is that classic, low-budget martial arts actioner that just isn't something anyone wants to finance anymore.
    - I think the tipping point was The Bourne Identity, along with the Jet Li/Jackie Chan/HK Martial Arts hype starting to die down. 

They proved that they can turn Matt Damon into a badass with frenetic editing.

EDIT: 

Actually this also coincides with the rise of the "grim dark" era where everyone wanted "gritty and realistic" action, and characters with more pathos.
3. ***Enough with the Clown!***

— He got his moment in the iconic *Dark Knight*, that’s more than most actors can say.
  - If I had a nickel for every time Michael Jai White appeared in a superhero movie and his enemy was an evil clown, I would have two nickels. Which is not a lot, but it is weird it happened twice.
4. Martial Arts movies in general are delegated to B movies, direct-to-DVD status in America at this point.

## 4. The Matrix to me

**Score:** 657

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Dark Knight
  - *Sharp music*

*Glass breaks*

*Zipline shoots*
    - *So why do they call him the Joker?*
2. Matrix.
  - I can hear the music that was over this in The Matrix.
3. Harry potter
4. Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory
5. Training Day
6. I agree, the Matrix
7. Scooby-Doo

## 5. Harold & Kumar go to White Castle. Yay or nay?

**Score:** 597

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s good. And it still holds up.
  - A lot of those early 2000s movies like Jay and silent Bob, Idiocracy, Beerfest. They are solid go-to movies when you want a good chuckle.
    - Let's add to this list, Grandma's Boy, Road Trip, Euro Trip, Step Brothers, Waiting, Superbad, Old School, Dodgeball, Super Troopers, Borat, Hot Rod, the Hangover l, Tropic Thunder, forgetting Sarah Marshall, Napoleon Dynamite, me myself and Irene, Zoolander... granted some were later 2000s movies we were so fucking spoiled
      - Dogma
2. Hard yay.
3. Yay
4. “Is this your special BUSH!!!!” 😂
5. *”Come on Pookie, let’s BURN this motherfucker down!!”*
6. I liked it.
7. This movie is the reason NPH is a thing today 

He got this before he got HIMYM

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. You really need to see Meshes of the Afternoon, a pioneering masterpiece of surrealism

**Score:** 64
**Body:** Maya Deren was one of the most important pioneers and champions of the American film avant-garde and experimental, and provided a blueprint for independent filmmakers seeking to escape commercial constraints of Hollywood. Directed together with Alexandr Hackenschmied, Meshes of the Afternoon (1943) changed the landscape of cinema with its exploration of the unconscious dream state. 

It puts you in a trance, that leaves you to muse on the nature of our subconscious psyche. 
Deren herself said "it does not record an event which could be witnessed by other persons. Rather, it reproduces the way in which the subconscious of an individual will develop, interpret and elaborate an apparently simple and casual incident into a critical emotional experience."

For those living in New York City, Meshes of the Afternoon and other short films by Deren is screening at Anthology Film Archives this coming Saturday, on 16mm. 'Meshes' will feature the score by Japanese-born composer Teiji Ito, who was Deren's husband towards the end of her life. 

https://www.anthologyfilmarchives.org/film_screenings/calendar?view=list&month=09&year=2025#showing-59959

It placed in the most recent Sight and Sound 2022 poll of the best films of all time, at #16.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Maya Deren is a must for anyone actually serious about cinema and you'll catch references to her work in many, many movies and across multiple genres.

Some of the most accessible avant garde work you get the honor of seeing.
  - Ahead of her time, perceptive, and thought-provoking

## 2. If Haneke was indeed moralizing about violence in media in "Funny Games", what's the problem?

**Score:** 48
**Body:**  The title is somewhat provocative, but I truly feel this way when I read criticism of this movie, like, for example, a recent post in this sub. The main criticism this movie and Haneke in general have received is that he's a moralizer. When they say this, I interpret it not in the sense that they think it's bad that he talks about a certain subject in a morally prescriptive way (as in, these are the correct attitudes to have and these are the bad ones), but that he has a particular attitude when touching morally sensitive topics, and this particular attitude is akin to him watching us from his ivory tower and lamenting how brutish we are, unlike him. The only way I could suscribe to that reductive way of experiencing his work is if he made inert, preachy movies, but he's anything but inert as a film-maker, and that goes particularly for "Funny Games".

 "Funny Games" is an emotionally captivating film. The characters suffering the violence are not mere vessels, but they actually feel like people, and the murderers are absolutely terrifying, particularly because they are aware that they are in a movie and are totally indulgent in the suffering they're causing, maybe because they know it's all fake. If Haneke was merely moralizing, I wouldn't find the film harrowing. And that's where I find the problems with this criticism of Haneke. It could be argued that he is moralizing, but he's an actually good director, so it works. If he was a bad director, he would have made generic melodramas. Watching "Funny Games" I'm drawn to reflect towards my own attitudes about suffering and the consumption of violence in media than I would be with a tear-jerker Hollywood drama that made the same point. What I want to say is this: is not enough to call him a moralist for it to lessen the film quality. There's good artistic moralizing and there's bad artistic moralizing. This is the former.

 Now, one may want to argue if the point he's making is good at all. "Funny Games" speaks about the way in which we consume violent media and become desensitized, or at least that's the interpretation of the movie that I'm interested in, and how most people seem to interpret it. He's not accusing you of the torture of this particular family, or, better said, he's not just accusing you of the torture of this family, but also about how come you found such a thing watchable in the first place, how did you watch all that violence before you watched this movie and how come you don't care anymore. 

 Empirically, there are some problems with this message. Violent games and movies don't seem to have made people actually more violent, because they are able to abstract themselves from the violence and enjoy some of its "liberating" aspects. You can enjoy "Kill Bill" but still be horrified if you saw a brawl in the street. One can watch people die in wars and still be disgusted, even if you've played and like many first person shooters. But, even if this is the case, is it still not troubling how we've become so accustomed to suffering? There are online communities dedicated to watching people meet their demise in horrible ways, be it torture or horrific accidents, even here in Reddit. And, before you say those are sick people, the fact is that all of the people that have ever watched this content are, sadly, not truly sick in the head, they can function normally in society like any human being, they can truly love others and be nice to people. And yet, they deliberately consumed content made by actual murderers, identifying in at least some way to them, finding them vessels for their enjoyment even when what they are doing is horrifying. Imagine being the victim of torture captured on video and knowing that some people are gonna watch that video to deliberately shock themselves for enjoyment. Morbid curiosity doesn't just affect the sick, everybody can attest to that. People that have ever watched these kinds of videos shouldn't be in jail, and most likely aren't even remotely close to replicate what they saw in those videos. And yet, isn't it all still horrifying? And isn't it worth it to have at least one big director who is concerned about that? That's why I don't mind if "Funny Games" is moralizing.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Funny Games is worth engaging with, but reveals how discussions & logic about violence in media is primarily treated as this singular confined topic. People never examine why so many love watching musicals, being immersed in them, yet have no real compulsion to sing or dance in their daily lives. People satisfied in committed relationships can enjoy stories about whirlwind affairs. Art has an alchemical power, focusing on one strain of it quickly makes for a desiccated conversation
2. "Funny Games" has stuck with me ever since I watched it a few years ago and while my feelings have vacillated on it, I've ultimately come to roughly agree with what you're saying here, which is that regardless of if you necessarily agree with the moralizing message, or even if you think that's what Haneke is trying to say it at all, ultimately it's just a well-crafted, emotionally effective film that gets you thinking about things few other movies even try to get you to. 

Reading your last paragraph helped elucidate my (as of now) final word on the issue, personally speaking. The thing is, humans have *always* been drawn to violence as spectacle; after all, look at the Roman gladiator fights! Violent movies (and other mass media) do not seem to have desensitized us, and do not seem to have empirically lead to more violence; we have always been at least somewhat desensitized. So regardless of authorial intent (I'm a big "death of the author" guy when it comes to interpretation), I think "Funny Games" does a good job of making me confront the part of me that's drawn to violence as spectacle, and what's scariest is that it's not something I can blame on watching too many Tarantino films; it's something that's always been there, or I wouldn't have wanted to watch movies like that in the first place.
3. To me the entire point 100% is that the movie is set up to make us love the moment when the bad guy gets shot. It is also the only violence we actually see enacted in real time and it feels good to us because the tortured victims are enacting the violence. Haneke is critical of violence as a solution sold to us in narratives. Fictional stories are written so that evil violence can be thwarted with good violence and now the evil violence is gone. This fallacy has fueled cycles of cruelty throughout history and is a lie we are exposed to constantly in the allowable types of violence in media. To me Haneke is showing a reaction we have as viewers but the anger is towards the movies that fuel that use that reaction to misrepresent the reality of cruelty.
4. When talking about the violence in Haneke’s movies Tarantino always seems to come up as well. I’m a big fan of both for their own merits but Tarantino’s mission statement is to entertain you and he does that. As for Haneke his messages tend to veer depending on the movie. He’s probably always passing judgement on folks watching violent content because it’s obscene in the first place. He’s not wrong but him taking such an authoritative stance is probably how he can hit the marks he can and I wouldn’t want him to change either. Him reducing Schindler’s List to entertainment is absurd but that thought process is probably how you get to Funny Games. 

I don’t know if I’m making any sense but I find Haneke fascinating especially when compared to his peers. 

There’s a YouTube video where he’s talking at a directors round table and he’s talking to John Krasinski about violence in movies. I can’t tell if the person who set those two up should be fired or promoted. The real entertainment would probably be Tarantino discussing movies with Haneke. Those two probably hate each other.
  - I love Haneke, but he's got this vibe sometimes where it feels like he's saying "we should all be really concerned about this thing!", while pointing his finger at the thing. I'm not sure how to explain it, but he just cares a lot about the topics he discusses. 

That's not a bad thing at all,but it feels like he wants to be in total control of the art he produces, which means the medium must answer for every element it represents on screen. Although it results in really tight and formally perfect movies, he projects a moral dimension to the consumption of art that is very... absurd. If not absurd, then at the very least, questionable.

Like you, I'm okay with it because it leads to incredibly interesting results like Caché or Funny Games, but if Haneke was trying to make a point about violence in the movies, it doesn't feel finished to me, personally.
5. I think the thing that a lot of people miss in their criticisms of Funny Games is that Haneke is implicating himself in the movie even more than he's implicating the audience. He is the provocateur -- he is the sadistic killers who toy with their subjects for their own entertainment. This adds an incredible dimension to the dynamic in the movie, IMO.
6. I think Funny Games takes up way too much oxygen in the discussion of Haneke, because IMHO it is one of his least interesting films. To me, Caché, Code Unknown, and The Seventh Continent are better illustrations of his thesis. To me, Haneke is fundamentally concerned with violence as it relates to questions of genre and narrative structure. His films are difficult to watch and actively antagonistic toward the viewer because he wants us to question our emotional investment in the catharsis provided by conventional narrative structure, and question whether we consume sensationalized violence on-screen as a means of distancing ourselves from the very real violence that surrounds us. 

A common motif in his films is domestic scenes in which the TV is constantly on in the background, often displaying real-life news scenes of violence and war. To me, this is slyly illustrating screen-based media's ability to evoke a "true" emotional reaction: we gasp in horror at the gruesome scenes happening in the foreground, while news of the very real terrors in the world drones on, barely puncturing our consciousness as anything other than background noise.

Anyway, that's just my interpretation, and why I think Haneke is a brilliant filmmaker whose work has only gotten more relevant over time. Honestly I found Funny Games to be one of his most moralizing films, which is why I didn't like it as much as his others.
  - I agree Funny Games is meant to provoke but The Piano Teacher is by far my favourite although I do think an argument could be made Amour is his masterpiece.
7. Michael Haneke has such a way with the camera and script that even if his attitude towards violence isn't the most approachable (like his think piece about Schindler's List), I find it darling to experience and appreciate as his voice is unlike just about any other. Plus his style of drama is right up my alley.
8. I don’t disagree with most of what you’re saying except this line: 

>If he was a bad director, he would have made generic melodramas.

Arthouse directors can be bad. Experimental filmmakers can be bad. It’s not like all the bad directors just happen to want to make the same kinds of films. Being a hack isn’t the only way to be bad.

## 3. A Serious Man Follow-Up Question: Embrace Simplicity and Stop Thinking

**Score:** 18
**Body:** As a follow-up to my post yesterday, A Serious Man has been racking my brain, and I can't help but think about the importance of the 'simplicity' message at the beginning and the parable of the Dentist from the second Rabbi. 

I feel like, in many ways, the Coen's main thesis of the film is that we need to stop being so hyper-cognizant about deep existential questions, as this is essentially a way to produce misery due to the absence of an answer. 

For instance, the opening quote about 'simplicity' is quite ironic considering the behaviour of Larry; he is anything but simple. He is incredibly intelligent and searching for answers to his suffering, and, of course, he isn't provided with an answer. I feel like the 'simplicity' in this case is exactly what Larry needs to embrace, something that is the antithesis of his intellectual probing. I feel Larry desperately needs to embrace the simplicity of Eastern philosophy, a philosophy focused on alleviating our conceptual overlay and thought. 

In addition, the parable of the dentist perfectly captures this intention. After the dentist discovers the religious message in the teeth, he plunges him into a state of neuroticism in an attempt to find answers. Eventually, after he stops asking questions, he gets on with life and seems to enjoy it. Essentially, he finds enjoyment again once he embraces simplicity and stops asking probing existential questions. In essence, he gets on with it, despite its meaninglessness. I couldn't help but think of Camus's absurdist hero.

## 4. Angst - An awkward, yet incredibly compelling film.

**Score:** 12
**Body:** 
This is why I love cinema. A narratively and structurally flawed film in theory, but absolutely mesmerising from start to finish. I’m struggling to pull my thoughts into a longer text for this one, so just wanted to highlight some of my initial thoughts:

**Spoilers: Some very light narrative spoilers, but mostly focused on mention of structure and themes.**


Maybe some of the absolute best camera work I’ve seen in a loooooong time. It basically made the film what it was, and I don’t think it would have anywhere near the impact without it. The static shots that suddenly move into following handycam. The claustrophobic inside and the expansive outside crane shots. The uncomfortable closeups and the chasing shots. Absolutely mesmerising. It made watching a guy clean up a house for 25 minutes feel like the opening of Saving Private Ryan.

When he first breaks into the house the camera takes this very high angle, following the killer, almost like a diorama, but giving you very little of anything outside his immediate vicinity. I wonder if this is to emphasise that he is the sole focus in the film, and the camera is essentially shutting out the rest of the house.

To support that, the other people in the film are basically set dressing. They get zero focus and characterisation, treated as objects in his world. They don’t even get a real voice and very little screen time.

I loved the calm, calculated, ominous voiceover giving you images of a meticulous, unfeeling sociopath, juxtaposed with the reality: A chaotic, uncoordinated, neurotic mess of a man who is driven by primal urges and impulse. 

Again relating to the characters. They don’t feel like real people. They don’t react or move like normal people do. They are quiet and void of personality. The people in the house don’t really contest what’s happening very much. The younger woman gets weirdly sexual at one point with him. The people at the cafe stare and move unnaturally. It reminded me of Yorgos Lanthimos movies where everyone is just a little bit off and uncanny. Very unsettling.

It’s an incredibly quiet film with a really subdued (but brilliant) Klas Schulze soundtrack. Which was a great surprise as didn’t know going in.

Sliding the girl across the floor into the kitchen? Erm. . . .

Got to be one of the best dogs in film, right? That little guy was a scene stealer and was in the film more than literally any other secondary character.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

You mentioned that the characters were devoid of personality, but I viewed that as a directorial choice where very few lines or directions were scripted for each character. That’s pure speculation on my part, but the way scenes developed felt like the director just gave them their motivations and said, “Action!”

As a result, we get a very visceral and surprisingly athletic performance from all involved in the house. If someone told me a few minor injuries occurred on set, I wouldn’t be surprised.

## 5. It regrets me to inform you all that The Officer and the Spy (2019) is good

**Score:** 6
**Body:** After watching the wonderfully tense procedural throwback [Conclave (2024)](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt20215234/), I had a look on IMDB to see what other films Robert Harris had worked on (I've yet to read any of his books which I've heard are also excellent).

What should I find but [An Officer and a Spy \(2019\)](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt2398149/?ref_=nm_knf_t_3), a film about the Dreyfus affair, a historical event that has always been of interest to me. I was surprised I'd never heard anyone talk about it.

So, after a little difficultly, I tracked down a copy from, ahem, a certain place on the high seas (it doesn't seem to be streaming anywhere locally which I found odd initially). 

And - what do you know? - it's good. It's very good.

The production values and staging are superb (it really does look like a 61 million Euro film), the performances are terrific across the board, the story is compelling and makes the interesting choice of starting the film at Alfred Dreyfus's degradation ceremony and exile to Devil's Island for the crime of disclosing military secrets to the Germans.

The protagonist - Lieutenant Colonel Georges Picquart - is not a likeable man. He's an unapologetic anti-semite with a deep respect for the traditions and honour of the French military. Picquart is a man who follows orders to the letter. Picquart is assigned to take over the Statistical Section (Military Intelligence), the same section that secured Dreyfus's conviction. Dreyfus happened to be Picquart's trainee in the academy, so he is very familiar with both the man and the case.

The intelligence service is wonderfully depicted as a sclerotic backwater formerly run by a corrupt and syphilitic Colonel. In his new post Picquart stumbles across information that proves Dreyfus's conviction was obtained on fraudulent grounds. Dreyfus is innocent.

This puts the Lieutenant Colonel in a deliciously difficult position. The army has no interest or desire to exonerate Dreyfus but Picquart, a man of honor, feels an obligation to the truth which culminates with him joining of forces with liberals like the writer Emile Zola, who goes on to publish his infamous *J'accuse* editorial based on Picquart's testimony.

Overall, it's a terrific depiction of what happens when individual injustice meets institutional irrationality and prejudice. The film is by no means perfect - it gets shaggy in the middle with the numerous trials and testimonies - but it concludes with a complex arc. Picquart may have felt an obligation to the truth and the honor of his beloved military but he felt no obligation toward actual justice or Dreyfus the man.

So, why the regret then? Well, it wasn't until the end of the film that I realized it had been directed by Roman Polanski. Yikes. And that, upon further reading, it seems that Polanski has been claiming kinship with Dreyfus during the film's press tour, based on his own legal "persecutions". Double yikes. 

That makes the film, which is undoubtedly  a good one, very difficult to recommend. I'm not sure whether or not I would have watched it had I known who directed it beforehand. But I thought it might make an interesting discussion more broadly on the ethics of watching a new film made by a person like Polanski or, say, Woody Allen. Apparently the film had its US premiere at a New York film festival just last month, six years after it was released, so the question is probably a timely one.

Is it okay to watch something like this so long as we don't pay for it? Or, given Polanski's noxious subtext, is it a form of propaganda best avoided all together? Clearly the decision whether to watch something like An Officer and a Spy comes down to individual scruples. Due to my ignorance and lack of research I wasn't forced to exercise mine. 

So what does your own conscience suggest might be the answer?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I have no problems separating the art from the artist even for someone like Roman Polanski, but I know many people do. I don’t think one could claim any moral high ground from any perspective. Several things are true: the man should have been convicted, but he was not. He is despicable for what he did, and Hollywood should have condemned his behavior instead of giving him standing ovations. But he also happens to be a good filmmaker, who has made films important to cinema history. 

It does make me wonder how much disgusting and revolting behavior have taken place in showbusiness that never reached public awareness. There are so many stories from Hollywood that remain untold.
2. I have no problem separating the art from the artist. If I like a director's films, I'll keep watching, even if I despise him as a person, whether for crimes or political opinions. Unfortunately, horrible people sometimes make good art, just as good people sometimes make horrible art, it happens. But it depends on each person's conscience, respect for people who choose not to watch films by problematic directors.
3. If you pirate a Polanski film and avoid telling the world you think it's great, you're in the clear. You get the film, he gets no money or credit. 

Whoops ...

Unfortunately Polanski is a very good filmmaker.
  - Yeah, you have a point. Should I delete you think or is it worth the discussion?
    - If anyone reading this is serious about improving the situation, here are two suggestions for advocacy in your jurisdiction:

1. Legal reforms to protect the rights of victims of rape and sexual assault to speak out about their experiences.

2. Legal reforms to enforce demonstration of "enthusiastic consent" as the standard to defend a rape charge.

It can be unfair to expect individual film industry workers, let alone film audiences, to solve the problem. They often have precarious employment reliant on the patronage of the very same highly networked echelon of the industry responsible for both the abusive behaviour, and the sanctimonious public defences of the likes of Polanski.
    - I don't mind the post at all. I don't think in this case that boycott will be an effective way of taking political action. And that is in no way a defence of Polanski, he's a rapist.

This is a filmmaker whose artistic reputation is assured, and whose notoriety is able to survive far stronger assaults, such as when Céline Sciamma and Adèle Haenel staged a walkout in protest against Polanski receiving an award at the 2020 Césars.

The controversy that surrounds Polanski is for two reasons: because he's made a lot of excellent films, and because the legal system and film industry, and societies on the whole, are generally very, very bad at convicting and punishing rich rapists, or any rapists for that matter. The reasons for that are manifold. 

The judgement that consumer action solves that problem has already fallen well short this decade, especially in France where the sanctimonious backlash has been huge.
